title: Jacea phrygia

There is no information available on the plant Jacea phrygia. However, Jacea phrygia is likely a misnomer, and it may be referring to the plant Inula helenium or the plant Inula phrygia (Helenium phrygium), or simply  Helenium phrygium,  which is a plant species in the daisy family (Asteraceae). 

Here's a paragraph on the plant Helenium phrygium (Inula phrygia):

Helenium phrygium (Inula phrygia) is a perennial plant species that is commonly known as Phrygian Inula or Golden Ragwort. The plant is valued for its medicinal properties, which include anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant effects, making it beneficial for treating various health conditions such as wounds, fever, and digestive issues. The bioactive constituents of Helenium phrygium include sesquiterpene lactones, inulin, and phenolic compounds. The plant is traditionally used in herbal medicine and is often made into teas, tinctures, and infusions, which are used to treat a range of health issues.
taxonomy: Jacea phrygia is classified within the plant family Asteraceae, commonly known as the daisy or sunflower family. The genus Jacea is a part of the tribe Senecioneae, which includes a group of flowering plants that are characterized by their daisy-like or composite flowers. The species Jacea phrygia is native to the Mediterranean region and is often found in dry, rocky areas. The plant is typically low-growing and produces small, yellow flowers. Its classification helps to understand the evolutionary relationships and characteristics of this and related plant species.

It is believed to have potential in treating various health conditions, including fever, cough, and respiratory issues.
